OpenText: With every passing example I'm souring quickly to the editorializing which reads a lot like unnecessary AI summarization. Trying to mentally discard this information and get to the actual content of the game itself is quickly becoming annoying.

Same feedback - too much flavor text. Too difficult to actually understand the game itself versus a subjective interpretation of what's going on. An example - "22:11 - Big action around the map! Near WoolyScout's Town Center, IcTiTaN's Elite Skirmishers and a Crossbowman were completely wiped out by a lone Knight and Builder defending, a massive loss for IcTiTaN! Simultaneously, WoolyScout's Crossbowmen are now aggressively raiding IcTiTaN's base, targeting his villagers! WoolyScout has also pushed out a Mangonel, while IcTiTaN is researching Leather Archer Armor." What I would say: "22:11 - WoolyScout killed 12 skirmishers and 6 crossbowmen with 1 knight (side note - excerpt mentions a "Builder" but I can't see a villager contributing here). WoolyScout continued with army of 15 crossbowmen and killed 3 villagers in IcTiTaN's base."

For me the editorializing (e.g., "what a push!" "strong play!" etc.) do not add to and rather distract from how easy it is to read the play-by-play and truly understand what's going on. I'd rather just read "Player 1 lost 3 skirmishers, Player 2 lost 5 skirmishers in this battle" kind of thing. This would make it easier to mentally track what's going on in the match. Also an oddity about "adding man-at-arms or militia" (something like that) after a drush with man at arm upgrade had already been declared was confusing.

I thought it was great that it identified what the map variance meant for the opening strategies of each player, however i wonder how much the forward gold was a factor, as it didnt examine other areas. I didnt get a good picture of the other resources, distance from each other etc. Also it would have been good to have had some extension of each of the players civs relationship to each other - so in addition to Britons: Strong archer civ Huns: Aggressive civ with no pop limit it would have been cool to have something like - Britons - their bonus to cheap tcs and archers suggests a defensive play but the huns have tarkans with high pierce armour which can counter archers (or words to that effect) Maybe something more about the players history with each other (i think thats available for comparison) For me, honestly i wouldnt read these for fun, enjoyment or learning - i would watch the visual streams - there is a lot more data being conveyed by this medium, however its great for when you have a tournament on and you cant commit to watching all the games - it works as a good summary of events.

This was better at highlighting the key points being contested but also said some things which are untrue - e.g. archers are not a counter to scouts and a market drop to age up isnt necessarily a focus on economy
Too long and wordy, i dont need to be welcomed to the game i just want the play by play condensed into the key actions
This was the best of the three - necessary and sufficient information only. It had more detail but less content which is ideal.
Dont drush with huns vs archer civs...
The description focused too much on the outcome of battles and not on the strategical or tactical decisions, which are more interesting
Too many exclamation marks and fake hype.
The intro of the match summary was too ambiguous to understand the situation, which essentially prompted me to skip most of the rest of the summary
Aztecs don't get cavalry archers, at least not without conversions. The text mentioned how the Aztec player lost CA but never mentions how they "appeared" in the first place
Not to be negative about the match summary but I prefer to watch a game rather than read about it

The few inconsistencies (e.g. crossbowmen before reaching castle age) realy threw me off. More significantly, it's way harder to get into the game without the visual, because well, that's how we know it. I think the base descriptions (the gold being forward top-left) should be either more succint (jsut "forward", the strategical info) or if more precise, then the army movements should also be traced more precisely. Also it would help with the visualisation if we were given the base positions on the minimap (so that we know if "top-left" is forward or backward).

In this one I think there's over-hyping at inappropriate moments (like when placing a new TC...). But again, I lacked a grasp of how the economies were comparing along the game, and what's the big picture looked like (e.g. when the Aztecs player lost their army in feudal, were they walled and on the way to castle age, ready to pike siege monk push, or fully opened and deep in feudal?). I did like the hyping moments for the big fights; I think ideally there would be an alternation of hype moments (big battle, red lost everything) and strategical insights (blue adds a TC after finding damage with the push, securing the eco advantage).

I couldn't get into it, I was lacking too much information to grasp enough what was happening to find it interesting. It's a strategy game, not a hype one, so I don't really care to learn that 3 skirms got killed, but rather how the battle changed or not the military balance, how much each player invested into it and how much it paid off, what's the status of the economies behind... Also I'm struggling to replace it in a real game, like from the updates it seemed to me that they had two small fights in feudal age, but then it was already time to click castle age even though almost nothing had happened. And then during the game I felt like the Vietnamese player was dominating, but all of a sudden they lost for an obscure reason.

(obvious from my answers) I much more related to those match updates; I'd say the players' intentions were better comunicated, with the strategical context, and the relatable situation. I'll just note that, regarding the questions about experiencing/improving from those match updates, I think there's a big layer of details that is (necessarily) missing from those updates but makes up the gaming (shape of woodline, where to quickwall, scouting pattern...) experience, hence why I don't think it'll work in that regard. Loved the introduction at the beginning
It's too boring to keep any kind of focus on it. Especially when it's not a match I care about. If this was between players I know or something like this I might be able to focus more.

It's very challenging keeping the state of the play based on small snippets like this. If it could be presented with for example a minimap, possibly with numbers referenced in the text it would be easier to understand where the various events are taking place.
It's simply impossible to follow and not interesting at all
Too few details

This one is significantly better than the previous one. It's way more compact and a lot of 'fluff' has been omitted. Still think a lot more could be removed though. For instance, I think you could question whether it's indeed useful information for a reader to know if a player's map is good or not without having FULL context at hand, and this would then of course just become way too long. Realistically, I think you could open: Point 1: Player A as Briton against Player B as Franks. Point 2: Player A opens 2 mil dr and Player B opens sc. Point 3: Player A harasses Player B's berries, Player B cleans it with their scouts, taking x amount of losses. Just try to write it as condensed as possible really.

Yeah so I think the sentencing is really off on certain places and if there is one thing that is really off-putting then it is the intense use of dramatic expressions. These match updates are like taking a transcript from MembTV (a very hyped up caster). This may work in a "live" environment, but absolutely not in text. Instead, I think it'd work much better if you keep it completely free of personal opinions and dramatic expressions. Would also be much better if you could condense more or less every single bullet point. Make it like sports update, basically.

Same as the first one really. It's like a transcript from a hype caster and it just doesn't work in paper form like this and even less so if it's live updates. I will refer back to the feedback provided on the second one.
It's getting better, this was more concise while presenting some necessary details. But, again, reading about aoe2 games is not my cup of tea.

AoE2 games are not meant to be read in blocks of text IMHO. Watching good casts is great, just listening to them in the background can even be nice, but reading about it lacks any of the energy and visual elements that make it fun. I wouldn't even read it if Tolkien himself wrote it 11. When it comes to this particular text, I particularly dislike the fact it just feels like a transcript of a rather poor caster. Too many exclamation marks, not enough context of the map etc.
This was better, but still it's just a slightly more eloquent take, using a medium that is just not right for experiencing an aoe2 game.
Again, this is not for me. Reading transcript-like text makes me reevaluate what I'm doing with my life.
Same comment as the previous example
There's no explanation of why actions were taken, and consequently no explanation of the strategic choices. At the tactical level, there's no explanation why engagements were won or lost.
Same comments as before
Same comments as before
good summary of what was happening. could be nice to have visual icons with some current stats on the side, low how many of what unit each had, what was lost in the skirmishes etc.
the tone was a little too optimistic in times when it was more even, it was a little too eager with it's adjectives. color coding the teams would help keep track of who is who.
seemed like more ai fluff
I appreciated the brevity, but i didn't know where the armys came from, or what they consisted of.

I didn't read this one as carefully. One definite time mismatch with knights and xbow before castle age. Overall, I thought it felt slightly less fluffed which I slightly preferred over the first passage even I think I gave this one slightly lower ratings.

Didn't feel like a human description of a match. Too much numerical detail on losses after each fight. Overly fluffy description of fights/players that don't contribute to an understanding of the actual flow of the match. Felt like a lot of reading for not a lot of useful info. Based purely on the description of the fights it seemed like the Franks player was going to win. In addition, the events described don't make sense for an actual game. Lots of inconsistency with how an actual game would be played like the Franks player teching into knights, cav archers, and longswords. Some of the events described also don't really seem to agree with provided timestamps. The sizes of the fights were weird.

I like that the updates were shorter. Felt more like an actual caster, fewer awkward word choices. The fights still don't really make sense, but was a better read. One thing that was worse was that there was no sense of when people were clicking up/advancing to the next age, which I think is pretty important for understanding the flow of the match. I also appreciate that they used drush to describe the early strategy instead of 3 militia + scout.
This one was worse. Too much hype/fluff becomes meaningless. Still not really a realistic match update in terms of what is happening. Indians are no longer a playable civ.

There is a repetition, for example, in description of how many units have been lost in engagement. This can be made livelier, or concrete. For some reason, I had trouble remembering the names (probably because they were unfamiliar to me). Finally, adding aspects of game that aren't usual and considered major would add colour and life to the game (blunders, sneaks, etc)
There was too much repetition of words and details and events with too little understanding of other aspects. Also had big trouble following this one
Honestly, by this time, it feels excessive to follow and understand a new match

Adding small major details about civilization in the beginning can be useful as one doesn't always remember. They can be applied to the situation (like Britons are an archer defensive civ due to ... And bad at open maps which might lead to some difficulty). The more I read, the more it seems a human eagle eyed view and narrative structure would improve the story.

sometimes i felt like there was some info missing, for example, how did huns player get an early vil lead if there wasnt vils killed? idle tc from enemy? maybe it needed a bit more of eco (vil count) updates overall, not just focused on military
this text felt a bit more messy, maybe because of the strat chosen by bulgarian player. but overall less descriptive than the first one

There were some repeated phrases. Some of the information did not make sense with the time stamps, for example: why was the second crush 2 minutes late and this wasn't commented on? The validity of continuing to make militia in feudal age was not commented upon and no mention of upgrades. The units lost weren't explained in detail in the opening segment... Villagers or military? It was too disjointed to be found useful or interesting

Once again, this game doesn't make sense! How did the men at arms pick off a farmer of all things when they attacked a woodline? It would have been in range of the TC and even a 1000 elo player would know not to make farms around the mill in early feudal. Once again, a lot of the engagements make no sense whatsoever... How did a force of 5 scouts take out 2 spearmen and some archers without sustaining losses? There is no immersion here and I cannot imagine these matches playing out like this at any but the lowest level.

Once again, this is written like it's from a game that doesn't exist. Why did players start with 5 vills? How would that impact the game? Why did players only have 25 vills at the time stamp? None of this makes any sense. Only one players opening was described, but how did the other respond? Why are militia still being made in feudal? This is either very low elo, in which case it's not useful, or completely made up

So many key details missing here. How many villagers were sent forward? Where was the counter tower placed? Why didn't the defender have an army ready to go? They should have had scouts or archers out by then. Were they full walling and trying to fc? Why did the attacker make men at arms and why did the archers not deal with these successfully? What were the ramifications of towering the gold? Another very incoherent game summary

The text was poorly written. also gave factually incorrect information. in arabia both sides start with 3 vils. not 5. and, regardless, expert players are going to start with 6 on food, not 5 Describing the starting gold and stone in the way that was chosen was, overly cumbersome. I would have gone with, both players have forward gold and stone, making it difficult to defend.. etc...
The counts of units lost is a distraction. Fights are either won, lost or units are traded

Franks would never go early castle knights and CA. That makes no sense. Also why does blue make jaguar warriors. You need a castle for them and they’re not part of the game until it’s the winning move? How?
A 17 minute tower rush game is never exciting. Sorry
Sorry, this was pointless after the first example.
I dont think a casting-style write-up works as text.

When it comes to the point "How would you rate the level of detail in the match updates?", as well as the match updates in general, my main criticism is that compared to human casters, the text provided boring, raw (and often hilariously incorrect in this case!) data. I want to hear about overall strategies and the outcome of an engagement without the text being bogged down by meaningless numbers. Who cares if player A lost 4 skirms and player B lost 2 skirms and a villager at the gold mine? I would prefer the updates to be more like "after an extended skirmisher war with losses on both sides, Player A surprised player B with an excellent mangonel shot, thereby taking the lead" or something along those lines. Currently, there's too much detail on things that don't actually matter.

Compared to the previous text, this one has different strengths and weaknesses. I understand there were engagements, but the impact of these engagements wasn't very well explained, the way a caster would. I did like that this felt like there were fewer updates, even if maybe there weren't. I found there was too little information on which age they were in and if they were going up or not. Reading about military count is useful, but where's the eco count? There also was some wrong information again (Aztec cav archers? Really?). If the text says something about engagements near a town center or on a hill, it would be great if the strategical impact of these plays would be written in there somewhere as well. Maybe mention resource counts or something?
I have little to add except I'm tired of reading bad examples that feel like AI-written slop. No offense meant, but please tell me you're putting this project to good use somehow?

This example could have used another update or two near the end to show the transition from defending the tower rush to a counterstrike, but other than that this was a halfway decent read if you understand aoe2. By FAR the best example text I've read so far. Fewer but longer updates focused on strategies and the outcomes of said strategies makes for a much nicer and easy-to-parse read. I like how information on player playstyle was added at the start. That's a very caster-y thing to do.

The texts were way too long, if I want to read a book, I'll read a book. It depends on the use case, but, e.g. if this is somewhat to summarize matches on a timeline for YouTube timestamps, it's too much. It'd rather prefer to have a short summary. I believe, on its own, these texts in general don't work really well. They should be combined with other media.
A bit too much text and filling words. But slightly better than the other texts before.
This is better, but I would still not read it instead of skipping through a video. I think they work well as an enhancement for A/V to find the right place to skip to, for example.
Reading this is for me not the right medium. I'd rather turn on text-to-speech, so I don't need to scroll downwards reading, what would be way more interesting seeing live.
I hate that I did this survey. How can I get back these 15 minutes of my life?

Boring to read and filled with a lot of emotional nonsense that didn't fit the events, very annoying, no competence in filtering important information, at the same time lack of crucial basic information such as villager numbers.
Tons of unnecessary information like concrete numbers of lost units or villager tasks while the key information is consistently missing.
How was that game over in the middle of an ongoing tower rush?
Units killed doesn't matter, it is the composition of the kills. If I have 20 xbows and 10 skirmishers in front, do I care if I lost the 10 skirmishers, they did their job as the meatshield.

Too much emphasis on Battle Events (I see what you are doing here, auto analyst), while completely ignoring the macro. Which player has upgrades, what is the eco like, is pressure on the gold villagers? Battle Events are not enough.
We need a eco indicator. Does one player have 20 villagers and the other 40? A villager kill is great but if you have not made a villager past 5 minutes then that one kill is not doing much.
Re-iterating my comment about Battle Events, they are not even half the game. Adding a 5 minute eco count would be helpful. Dude#1 has 30 villagers, 15 on farms, 10 on wood, 2 builders and 3 idle.

Lacks any overarching strategic insights into why actions were taken, individual skirmish unit losses are irrelevant without context. Focused far too much on minor details without considering gamestate, no pointing out missed opportunities/mistakes. Overall incredibly poor standard relative to current caster quality
Excessive fake hype ruined it. There is a reason memb is considered the worst caster (outside of his narcissism)
Improved, additional context was beneficial. Didn't go far enough.
Far better

this one had much more useful information, i felt like i was communicated a template of a game i've seen and played often. still, it just seems really difficult to communicate game state through text, compared to something like basic visual information and score and pop

to understand how a match is going, repeatedly reading that player x won a fight isn't very useful to me. Useful information that I use to parse what's happening (e.g. when jumping into a cast or rec): pop, vill count, score, ages, what the minimap looks like

again focus on the wrong things for me. lots of unique fights that i don't really need to know about. suddenly a fight involves elite skirm (that a player is aging up, that would have been useful info!), it's impossible to judge who's in a good position

it's nice that it's shorter and focused a bit more on the res positions. but i don't feel like i can actually envision the game described, because to gauge who's winning (especially in a brittle strat like tower rush), you need much more information than that another tower was placed or similar. i feel like it doesn't help me update my mental image of the game, and when one player loses it comes completely out of left field
Info about counter raid missing

I know your e-mail adres is dutch, and being dutch too, i tried to come up with a better football analogy but i'm a baseball fan so here it is. I'm comparing this with the game logs that are provided of baseball games. To see an example, go to mlb.com, hover over any completed game and click 'wrap'. That will give the game log on the right hand side. You can read how someone flew out to center field, or how they got a base hit. But while the basic info is there, i don't think it comes close to actually watching and experiencing the game, or even highlights of the game. I had the same feeling with the match updates i just read.
I feel like this was the best one of the 4, although there were still a few too many 'hype' moments ending in a ! that really shouldnt be.
This match update seemed more standardized, with all phrases ending with a !. I don't think that made it better to read, in fact the phrasing is somewhat worse.
The number of TCs each side dropped would have helped understand the time pressures they are under to get damage at different points in the game

You need to understand the game state to know what is at stake at any one moment, eg what are the vill counts, how close to going up is someone, have they gone to stone to drop more counter tower etc These small choices are what i find most interesting
At the start, a paragraph appears twice
There's still an eruption in there 😠 Also, many times, the commentary only tells us the outcome of battles, but not when a battle is happening
Awful choice of words, like an LLM that is too excited. Battles don't "erupt" in aoe2. Also, the impact of losses frequently seems to be rather exaggerated.
You put "Strongly agree" at the top as well as the bottom on the previous page, you silly goose
The ending is pretty abrupt
Too short and unstructured

Neither the players nor the caster read as very competent here. There's large gaps with absolutely no discussion going on, followed by a lot of noise about individual units dying and other excessively detailed information that generally isn't really very necessary. It sort of reads like a worse version of "Memb style casting", like it's trying to be hype, but gets bogged down too much with "IronBoar lost only 2 Crossbows but TC_Eater lost 23 vills". There are also some things that really stood out to me as the players not being very competent, namely: Losing three archers to a lumberjack, that should never happen, playing a lot of militia as Huns (especially going into Feudal), and TC_Eater not falling back after being late to Castle Age. There were also a few things that made the caster seem rather inexperienced, these being talking about Longbows just because IronBoar has started making archers (in a way that authoritatively states that Longbows will happen, when they likely won't), and the mistake with saying TC_Eater lost three Crossbows but then clicks up to Castle Age (implying they lost Castle Age units they wouldn't yet have access to).

These players definitely feel higher level, though there's still some things that are weird (like the random unit transitions from red). The casting could do with some improvement though, there's no mention of possible strats (same for all the games so far), unit transitions are not foreshadowed at all (no mention of when the player starts adding a certain unit type, they just suddenly appear), and excessively using the player color + player name combined is unnecessary and a bit annoying. I also preferred when the positions of the player resources were better described, as that lets me better understand the map appearance (could be good to know the rough position on the map of players as well).

It's probably because I've read three of these now, but it's starting to feel less like these are proper casts, due to the lack of AoE style language. For example, Elite Skirmishers could often just be called "Skirms", Mangonels to "Mangos" etc. There's still a bit too much granularity in the precise number of units given (people don't really need to know that exactly 49 units died at the end for example). The players still don't read as pros, maybe 12-1400 elo. They were quite late to add any military, and made some weird choices (even by older game meta). There's also a lot of excessive army swings, but it does kind of make it more interesting in a way.

I still wouldn't consider these players pros by the sound of it, but it might also be the caster just missing a lot of info. CastleDropper going forward with vills should've been mentioned, as well as any forward buildings or generally the militia. I can follow along because I know how games like this go, but there was still a good bit of missing info (the militia line suddenly appeared when they mostly died), and we were not really informed as to the units being made by either player. Also would've been good to know where the towers were built in terms of resources.
